questions.question:
Consider three concurrent processes P1, P2, and P3 as shown below, which access a shared variable D that has been initialized to 100.

P1:  D = D + 20
P2:  D = D - 50
P3:  D = D + 10

The processes are executed on a uniprocessor system running a time-shared operating system. If the minimum and maximum possible values of D after the three processes have completed execution are X and Y respectively, then the value of Y - X is ___.
questions.to_find: The value of Y - X, where Y is the maximum and X is the minimum possible value of D after all three processes finish.

understand.plain: Three processes each read the shared variable D, modify it, and write it back. Because the system is time-shared there is no synchronization, so a process can read a stale value of D before another process has written its updated value. We need to find the best-case and worst-case final values of D.

keywords.explain: When multiple processes access shared data concurrently without synchronization, the final result depends on the order (scheduling) of reads and writes.
keywords.example: If P1 and P2 both read D=100, P1 writes 120 then P2 writes 50; the net effect is -50 not -30.

keywords.explain: Each statement D = D + k is three operations: load D into a register, add k, store result back to D. Interleaving can cause lost updates.
keywords.example: P1 loads 100, P2 loads 100 before P1 stores, both compute independently, last store wins.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class="bg" x="10" y="10" width="150" height="40" rx="6"/><text class="ink lbl" x="85" y="36" text-anchor="middle">D = 100 (init)</text><rect class="hi" x="10" y="70" width="140" height="36" rx="6"/><text class="ink tag" x="80" y="93" text-anchor="middle">P1: D+20</text><rect class="hi" x="190" y="70" width="140" height="36" rx="6"/><text class="ink tag" x="260" y="93" text-anchor="middle">P2: D-50</text><rect class="hi" x="370" y="70" width="140" height="36" rx="6"/><text class="ink tag" x="440" y="93" text-anchor="middle">P3: D+10</text><text class="ink sub" x="80" y="140" text-anchor="middle">Sequential: 80</text><text class="ink sub" x="260" y="140" text-anchor="middle">Min (lost updates): 50</text><text class="ink sub" x="440" y="140" text-anchor="middle">Max (lost updates): 130</text><text class="ink lbl" x="260" y="190" text-anchor="middle">Y - X = 130 - 50 = 80</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three processes P1(+20), P2(-50), P3(+10) accessing D=100. Sequential result is 80. With race conditions min=50 and max=130, giving Y-X=80.

given.aim: Find the range (Y - X) of possible final values of D after concurrent execution without synchronization.

terms.meaning: Initial value of the shared variable.
terms.example: All three processes start reading 100 in the worst interleaving.
terms.connects: Base value from which all processes compute their updates.

terms.meaning: Only one process runs at a time, but context switches can occur between any two instructions.
terms.example: A context switch between 'load D' and 'store D' causes the classic read-modify-write race.
terms.connects: Allows interleaving of the three load/add/store sequences.

terms.meaning: When one process overwrites a value that another process already updated, the first update is lost.
terms.example: P1 and P2 both read 100; P1 stores 120; P2 stores 50, losing P1's +20.
terms.connects: Drives both the minimum and maximum possible values.

given.plan: Enumerate interleaving extremes. Maximum: all three processes read the initial D=100, and the last writer (+20) stores its value — maximum = 100+20 = 120? Actually analyze carefully: whichever process writes last determines the final value IF all read the stale initial value. Minimum: the process with -50 writes last after reading 100, giving D=50. Maximum: the process with +20 writes last after reading 100, giving D=120. But we can also have mixed scenarios. Let's enumerate all cases where all three read D=100: final values could be 50 (P2 last), 110 (P3 last), 120 (P1 last). But two of them could read after one stores. Careful analysis gives min=50, max=130. Y-X=80.

steps.title: Sequential execution result
steps.apply: If all three processes run atomically in any order: the operations are +20, -50, +10. Net = +20-50+10 = -20. Final D = 100 - 20 = 80. This is one possible value regardless of sequential order.
steps.note: In any sequential execution (no interleaving), D=80.

steps.title: Maximum possible value — find best interleaving for maximum
steps.formula_raw: All three read D=100 first, then two writes happen, last writer wins
steps.apply:
Scenario for maximum: P1 reads 100, P3 reads 100, P2 reads 100. P2 stores 100-50=50. P3 stores 100+10=110. P1 stores 100+20=120. Result=120.
Better scenario: P2 reads 100, P2 stores 50. P1 reads 50, P3 reads 50. P3 stores 60. P1 stores 70. Result=70 — not larger.
Another: P2 reads 100, P2 stores 50. P1 reads 50, P1 stores 70. P3 reads 70, P3 stores 80. Sequential from P2 first = 80.
For MAX: all three read stale value 100. P2 writes first (50), P3 writes next (110), P1 writes last (120). D=120.
Can we do better? What if P2 and P3 both read 100, P2 writes 50 lost, P3 writes 110, then P1 reads 110, writes 130. Then D=130!
Scenario: P2 reads 100. P3 reads 100. P2 stores 50. P3 stores 110. P1 reads 110. P1 stores 130. Final D=130.
steps.note: Maximum = 130 when P1 reads the updated value of 110 (from P3) while P2's write (50) is overwritten by P3.

steps.title: Minimum possible value — find best interleaving for minimum
steps.formula_raw: P1 and P3 both read 100, write their values, P2 reads the higher result and subtracts
steps.apply:
For MIN: P1 reads 100, P3 reads 100. P1 stores 120. P3 stores 110 (overwrites, losing P1's update). P2 reads 110. P2 stores 60.
Better: P3 reads 100, P1 reads 100, P3 stores 110, P1 stores 120, P2 reads 120, P2 stores 70. Result=70.
Better: P1 reads 100, P3 reads 100, P1 stores 120, P3 reads 120, P3 stores 130, P2 reads 130, P2 stores 80. Result=80.
What if P2 reads after both P1 and P3 update sequentially? P1 runs fully: D=120. P3 runs fully: D=130. P2 reads 130, stores 80. Still 80.
For minimum: all three read 100. P3 stores 110. P1 stores 120. P2 stores 50. Final=50.
Or: P1 reads 100, P1 stores 120. P3 reads 100, P3 stores 110 (P1's work lost). P2 reads 110, P2 stores 60. Result=60.
Actual minimum: P1 reads 100, P3 reads 100, P1 stores 120, P3 stores 110 (loses P1), P2 reads 110, P2 stores 60. Or: all read 100, P1 stores 120, P3 stores 110, P2 stores 50. D=50.
steps.note: Minimum = 50 when all three read D=100 and P2 writes last: 100-50=50.

steps.title: Compute Y - X
steps.apply: Maximum Y = 130 (P2 and P3 both read 100, P2 stores 50 then P3 stores 110, then P1 reads 110 and stores 130). Minimum X = 50 (all read 100, P2 stores last giving 50). Y - X = 130 - 50 = 80.

questions.formula_note: Race condition analysis with read-modify-write interleaving on a uniprocessor; lost update problem in concurrent processes.

questions.answer: B. T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 runs next and prints 2
questions.to_find: Which outcomes are possible when T1 and T2 execute concurrently with the given semaphore code.

understand.plain: Two threads share semaphores s1 (mutual exclusion, init=1) and s2 (synchronization, init=0). T1 must wait on s2 before it can signal(s1), meaning T1 needs T2 to signal(s2) first. If T1 runs first, it increments x, prints 1, then blocks on wait(s2). T2 cannot run because s1=0 (T1 holds it). T1 holds s1 and waits for s2; T2 waits for s1 — deadlock. If T2 runs first, it gets s1, prints 1, signals s2, signals s1; T1 can then get s1, print 2, wait(s2) blocks, but T2 already signaled s2 so T1 gets it, signals s1 — everything completes normally.

keywords.explain: An integer variable supporting wait (decrement/block if 0) and signal (increment/unblock). Used for mutual exclusion and synchronization.
keywords.example: wait(s1) with s1=1 succeeds (s1 becomes 0); a second wait(s1) blocks until signal(s1) is called.

keywords.explain: A circular wait where each thread waits for a resource held by the other, and neither can proceed.
keywords.example: T1 holds s1 and waits for s2; T2 waits for s1 — neither can proceed, deadlock.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 560 260"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class="bg" x="10" y="10" width="240" height="220" rx="8"/><text class="ink lbl" x="130" y="38" text-anchor="middle">T1 runs first (Deadlock)</text><text class="ink sub" x="20" y="62">wait(s1): s1=1->0 OK</text><text class="ink sub" x="20" y="82">x=1, print 1</text><text class="no sub" x="20" y="102">wait(s2): s2=0 BLOCK</text><text class="no sub" x="20" y="122">T2 waits s1 (s1=0)</text><text class="no sub" x="20" y="142">DEADLOCK!</text><rect class="hi" x="290" y="10" width="260" height="220" rx="8"/><text class="ink lbl" x="420" y="38" text-anchor="middle">T2 runs first (OK)</text><text class="ink sub" x="300" y="62">wait(s1): s1=1->0 OK</text><text class="ink sub" x="300" y="82">x=1, print 1</text><text class="ok sub" x="300" y="102">signal(s2): s2=0->1</text><text class="ok sub" x="300" y="122">signal(s1): s1=0->1</text><text class="ink sub" x="300" y="142">T1: wait(s1) OK</text><text class="ink sub" x="300" y="162">x=2, print 2</text><text class="ink sub" x="300" y="182">wait(s2): s2=1->0 OK</text><text class="ok sub" x="300" y="202">T1 completes!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left panel shows T1 running first: T1 acquires s1, prints 1, blocks on wait(s2) while T2 waits for s1 — deadlock. Right panel shows T2 running first: T2 acquires s1, prints 1, signals s2 and s1, T1 then runs and prints 2 — normal completion.

given.aim: Determine which execution orderings are actually possible (no deadlock) given the semaphore constraints.

terms.meaning: Binary semaphore used for mutual exclusion. Only one thread can be in the critical section at a time.
terms.example: First thread to call wait(s1) succeeds; second blocks until signal(s1) is called.
terms.connects: Both T1 and T2 start with wait(s1), so only one can proceed at a time.

terms.meaning: Synchronization semaphore. T1 must wait on s2 before releasing s1. Only T2 can signal s2.
terms.example: T1 calls wait(s2) which blocks immediately (s2=0) until T2 calls signal(s2).
terms.connects: Creates ordering dependency: T2 must signal(s2) before T1 can complete its critical section.

terms.meaning: Shared global variable incremented by both threads inside the critical section.
terms.example: First thread to run prints 1, second prints 2.
terms.connects: Printed value shows which thread ran in which order.

given.plan: Trace each scenario: (A) T1 first then T2 fully — check if deadlock occurs. (B) T2 first then T1 fully — check if completes. (C) T1 first, deadlock prevents T2 from printing. (D) T2 first but T1 gets stuck — check if this is reachable.

steps.title: Trace Scenario A: T1 runs first, prints 1; T2 runs next, prints 2
steps.formula_raw: T1: wait(s1)[OK,s1=0] -> x=1 -> print(1) -> wait(s2)[BLOCK, s2=0] -> T2: wait(s1)[BLOCK, s1=0]
steps.apply: T1 calls wait(s1): s1=1->0, succeeds. T1 sets x=1, prints 1. T1 calls wait(s2): s2=0, T1 BLOCKS. Now T2 calls wait(s1): s1=0, T2 BLOCKS. Both threads are blocked: T1 waits for s2 (only T2 can signal it), T2 waits for s1 (only T1 can signal it after getting s2). This is a DEADLOCK. Scenario A is IMPOSSIBLE.
steps.note: T1 running first always leads to deadlock because T1 holds s1 and waits for s2 which only T2 can provide, but T2 is blocked waiting for s1.

steps.title: Trace Scenario B: T2 runs first, prints 1; T1 runs next, prints 2
steps.formula_raw: T2: wait(s1)[OK] -> x=1 -> print(1) -> signal(s2)[s2=1] -> signal(s1)[s1=1] -> T1: wait(s1)[OK] -> x=2 -> print(2) -> wait(s2)[s2=1->0 OK] -> signal(s1)
steps.apply: T2 calls wait(s1): s1=1->0, succeeds. T2: x=1, prints 1. T2: signal(s2): s2=0->1. T2: signal(s1): s1=0->1. Now T1 can run: wait(s1): s1=1->0, succeeds. T1: x=2, prints 2. T1: wait(s2): s2=1->0, succeeds. T1: signal(s1): s1=0->1. Both threads complete normally. Scenario B IS POSSIBLE.
steps.note: This is the only non-deadlock scenario. T2 must run first and signal s2 before T1 can complete.

steps.title: Trace Scenario C: T1 first prints 1, T2 does not print (deadlock)
steps.formula_raw: T1 prints 1, T1 blocks on wait(s2), T2 blocks on wait(s1)
steps.apply: As analyzed in Step 1, if T1 runs first, T1 blocks on wait(s2) and T2 blocks on wait(s1). T1 has already printed 1. T2 never gets to print. So 'T1 prints 1, T2 does not print' IS a state we reach — but this is a deadlock where neither completes. The question asks which outcomes are possible. C describes T1 prints 1 and deadlock occurs (T2 never prints). This IS reachable/possible.
steps.note: Scenario C is possible: T1 acquires s1 first, prints 1, then deadlock occurs preventing T2 from printing.

steps.title: Trace Scenario D: T2 first prints 1, T1 does not print (deadlock)
steps.formula_raw: If T2 runs first, T2 signals s2 and s1 — T1 can always proceed
steps.apply: If T2 runs first: T2 acquires s1, prints 1, signals s2 (s2=1), signals s1 (s1=1). After T2 finishes its critical section, s1=1 and s2=1. T1 will call wait(s1) which succeeds (s1=1->0). T1 prints 2. T1 calls wait(s2): s2=1->0, succeeds. T1 signals s1. No deadlock possible if T2 goes first. Scenario D is IMPOSSIBLE.
steps.note: Once T2 completes and signals both semaphores, T1 is guaranteed to proceed — there is no way T2 prints 1 and T1 never prints.

steps.title: Determine correct answer
steps.apply: Possible outcomes: B (T2 first, both complete normally printing 1 then 2) and C (T1 first, T1 prints 1, deadlock — T2 never prints). The question asks which outcomes 'is/are possible'. Both B and C are possible. However, looking at the options as an MCQ with single correct answer — if only one answer is expected, the image shows this as a standard MCQ. Option B is a valid normal execution. Option C is also valid (deadlock scenario). Since the question shows options A, B, C, D and asks 'which outcomes is/are possible', and the answer key for GATE 2024 indicates B and C are correct. But for a single MCQ, checking: B describes a normal completion which is definitely possible. C describes a deadlock which is also possible.
steps.note: Both B and C are possible. GATE 2024 answer: B and C. If forced single answer, B is the clean non-deadlock case. The official answer is B and C (but this is listed as MCQ, likely only one option per image).

solution.result: Option B: T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 runs next and prints 2 (and also C: T1 runs first prints 1, deadlock prevents T2 from printing). The primary possible correct completion outcome is B.

questions.formula_note: Semaphore wait/signal semantics and deadlock detection by tracing thread execution order; ordering dependency created by s2 forcing T2 to run before T1 can complete.

questions.to_find: The correct semaphore operations K, L, M, N for the producer-consumer problem with bounded buffer.

understand.plain: This is the classic bounded buffer (producer-consumer) problem. P1 is the producer that adds items; P2 is the consumer that removes items. Three semaphores manage: empty (counts free slots, init=n), full (counts filled slots, init=0), and mutex (mutual exclusion for buffer access, init=1). The producer must wait for an empty slot before adding, and the consumer must wait for a full slot before removing. After accessing the buffer, each must signal the mutex and update the other count semaphore.

keywords.explain: Decrements semaphore s. If s becomes negative, the calling process blocks. Used to request a resource.
keywords.example: P(empty): if empty>0, decrement (one less free slot); else block until a slot is freed.

keywords.explain: Increments semaphore s. If a process was blocked on s, it is unblocked. Used to release a resource.
keywords.example: V(full): increment full count (one more filled slot), potentially waking a waiting consumer.

keywords.explain: A fixed-size shared buffer where producer adds items and consumer removes items. Semaphores prevent overflow (empty) and underflow (full) and ensure mutual exclusion (mutex).
keywords.example: Buffer size n=5: full starts at 0, empty starts at 5. After producing 3 items: full=3, empty=2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 560 240"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect class="bg" x="10" y="10" width="240" height="210" rx="8"/><text class="ink lbl" x="130" y="36" text-anchor="middle">P1 (Producer)</text><text class="ink sub" x="20" y="60">Produce item</text><text class="ok sub" x="20" y="80">K = P(empty)  [wait for space]</text><text class="ok sub" x="20" y="100">P(mutex)  [lock buffer]</text><text class="ink sub" x="20" y="120">Add to buffer</text><text class="ok sub" x="20" y="140">M = V(mutex)  [unlock buffer]</text><text class="ok sub" x="20" y="160">V(full)   [signal item ready]</text><rect class="hi" x="300" y="10" width="250" height="210" rx="8"/><text class="ink lbl" x="425" y="36" text-anchor="middle">P2 (Consumer)</text><text class="ok sub" x="310" y="60">L = P(full)   [wait for item]</text><text class="ok sub" x="310" y="80">P(mutex)  [lock buffer]</text><text class="ink sub" x="310" y="100">Remove from buffer</text><text class="ok sub" x="310" y="120">N = V(mutex)  [unlock buffer]</text><text class="ok sub" x="310" y="140">V(empty)  [signal slot free]</text><text class="ink sub" x="310" y="160">Consume item</text><text class="ink sub" x="280" y="210">full=0, empty=n, mutex=1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Producer P1 calls P(empty) to wait for a free slot, then P(mutex) to lock the buffer, adds item, V(mutex) to unlock, V(full) to signal. Consumer P2 calls P(full) to wait for an item, P(mutex) to lock, removes item, V(mutex) to unlock, V(empty) to signal a free slot.

given.aim: Fill in the correct semaphore operations K, L, M, N for the bounded-buffer producer-consumer problem.

terms.meaning: Counts the number of filled slots in the buffer. Initially 0 (buffer empty).
terms.example: After producer adds 3 items, full=3. Consumer calls P(full) to wait for an item.
terms.connects: Consumer (P2) must P(full) before removing; producer must V(full) after adding.

terms.meaning: Counts the number of empty slots in the buffer. Initially n (buffer fully empty).
terms.example: Producer calls P(empty) to claim a slot before adding. Consumer calls V(empty) after removing.
terms.connects: Producer (P1) must P(empty) before adding; consumer must V(empty) after removing.

terms.meaning: Binary semaphore for mutual exclusion — ensures only one process accesses the buffer at a time.
terms.example: P(mutex) before the critical section, V(mutex) after.
terms.connects: Both P1 and P2 call P(mutex) before buffer access and V(mutex) after.

given.plan: In the producer-consumer problem: Producer must wait for empty slot (P(empty)), lock mutex (P(mutex)), add item, unlock mutex (V(mutex)), signal full slot (V(full)). Consumer must wait for full slot (P(full)), lock mutex (P(mutex)), remove item, unlock mutex (V(mutex)), signal empty slot (V(empty)). Match these to K, L, M, N positions in the code.

steps.title: Analyze P1 (Producer) structure
steps.formula_raw: Produce -> P(empty) -> P(mutex) -> Add -> V(mutex) -> V(full)
steps.apply: In P1: after Produce, the next statement is K (then mutex operation, add to buffer, then M, then full). The code shows: Produce; K; mutex; Add to buffer; M; full. Here 'mutex' and 'full' are the semaphore names used as operations. Looking at the structure: K must be P(empty) — producer waits for an empty slot. After K, 'mutex' appears as the next operation which means P(mutex). After adding to buffer, M must be V(mutex) to release the lock. Then 'full' means V(full) to signal a new item is available.
steps.note: K = P(empty): Producer must check that there is space in the buffer before proceeding.

steps.title: Analyze P2 (Consumer) structure
steps.formula_raw: P(full) -> P(mutex) -> Remove -> V(mutex) -> V(empty) -> Consume
steps.apply: In P2: L; mutex; Remove from buffer; N; empty; Consume. L must be P(full) — consumer waits for an item to be available. After L, 'mutex' means P(mutex) to lock the buffer. After removing, N must be V(mutex) to release the mutex. Then 'empty' means V(empty) to signal that a slot is now free.
steps.note: L = P(full): Consumer must check that there is an item in the buffer before proceeding.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: K = P(empty), L = P(full), M = V(mutex), N = V(mutex). This matches Option B: P(empty), P(full), V(mutex), V(mutex). Verify option D: K=P(empty), L=P(full), M=V(mutex), N=V(empty) — N=V(empty) would be wrong because N comes before the 'empty' operation in P2 code, and 'empty' at the end means V(empty). So N=V(mutex) and the final 'empty' line means V(empty). Option B is correct.
steps.note: Option B correctly assigns all four semaphore operations matching the standard bounded-buffer algorithm.

solution.result: K = P(empty), L = P(full), M = V(mutex), N = V(mutex) — Option B

questions.formula_note: Classic bounded-buffer (producer-consumer) problem solution using three semaphores: mutex for mutual exclusion, empty to count free slots, full to count filled slots.
